claims.text: Alexander Tom, a Scottish engineer and Oxford professor, identified a precise 2.72 ft unit he called the megalithic yard used at hundreds of ancient stone sites across Britain and France.

evidence.quote:
Alexander Tom, a Scottish engineer,
an Oxford professor

A single unit of
measurement, exactly 2.72 ft.

claims.rationale: Alexander Thom (spelling varies) was a real Oxford engineering professor whose decades of megalithic surveys are documented in his 1967 book 'Megalithic Sites in Britain'. The 2.72 ft figure and 'megalithic yard' terminology are his. The claim describes established fact about Thom's empirical work.

claims.text: Tom surveyed more than 600 megalithic sites across Britain and France over decades of fieldwork.

evidence.quote:
he studied
more than 600 megalithic sites across
Britain and
France

claims.rationale: Well-documented biographical fact. Thom's survey corpus across Britain, France (Carnac especially) and Orkney is cataloged in his published site gazetteers.

dependencies.rationale: The survey corpus is the empirical foundation that led Thom to posit the megalithic yard.

claims.text: The Ring of Brodgar in Scotland was originally 60 stones arranged in a near-perfect circle of 340 ft diameter, with placement accurate to within half an inch.

evidence.quote:
The ring of Broadgar in
Scotland has 27 stones set in a perfect
circle, but it was originally 60 stones.

Stone placement accurate to
within half an inch over a 340 ft
diameter

claims.rationale: The Ring of Brodgar's original 60-stone count and ~104m diameter are mainstream archaeology. The 'half-inch accuracy' over 340 ft is Thom's measurement; modern resurveys (e.g. by Clive Ruggles) find somewhat less perfection but still impressive precision.

claims.text: The perimeter of Stonehenge's Sarsen circle measures exactly 120 megalithic yards, with each stone 2.5 megalithic yards wide.

evidence.quote:
The perimeter of the
Sarsson circle at Stonehenge measures
exactly 120 megalithic yards.

claims.rationale: Thom's proposed fits. Modern resurveys show the sarsen circle perimeter is close to but not exactly 120 megalithic yards; the claim depends on which measurement datum you use and how much you round.

dependencies.rationale: Stonehenge's dimensional fit is one of Thom's marquee examples supporting the megalithic-yard hypothesis.

claims.text: Neolithic builders used Pythagorean triangles in their stone layouts thousands of years before Pythagoras was born.

evidence.quote:
Evidence of Pythagorean triangles
thousands of years before Pythagoras was
born.

claims.rationale: Some Neolithic structures exhibit 3-4-5 or similar integer-ratio triangles, and knowledge of such ratios plausibly predates Pythagoras (who formalized the theorem, he did not discover the triangles). Whether builders 'used' the theorem intentionally is a stronger claim — the empirical right-angle fits are real.

claims.text: Some Neolithic stone circles were precise enough to predict lunar eclipses and tracked the moon's 18.6-year standstill cycle.

evidence.quote:
Some sites were precise enough to
predict lunar eclipses.

claims.rationale: Some astronomical alignments (solstice sunrise/sunset, lunar standstill directions) at Stonehenge, Callanish and Maeshowe are well-established in mainstream archaeoastronomy. Eclipse prediction via the Aubrey Holes is a hypothesis (Fred Hoyle, Gerald Hawkins) with partial support — not consensus.

claims.text: Stonehenge, Avebury and Glastonbury Tor form an isosceles triangle with 19,000-megalithic-yard long sides at exactly 23.5° — matching Earth's axial tilt.

evidence.quote:
Stonehenge, Avery, Glassenbury to they
formed an isosles triangle.

the angles between
them are
23.5°. That's the same angle of the
Earth's axial tilt.

claims.rationale: Classic cherry-picked-geometry claim from alternative-archaeology literature. With enough candidate sites (hundreds) one will always find triangles matching any chosen angle. Host's own verdict later endorses this criticism.

claims.text: Stonehenge, Silbury Hill and Old Sarum form a 3-4-5 Pythagorean right triangle with whole-number megalithic-yard sides.

evidence.quote:
Stonehenge, Silbury Hill, and Old
Sar. The sides form a 345 Pythagorean
triangle.

claims.rationale: Same selection-bias problem. Many trios of prominent English sites will produce near-right triangles; exact 3-4-5 proportions in whole megalithic yards require tolerant rounding.

claims.text: The megalithic alignment triangles can only be seen from high altitude or orbit, implying Neolithic builders had knowledge requiring aerial survey capability.

evidence.quote:
These alignments
weren't visible from the ground. They
can only be seen from high above

claims.rationale: The premise (that alignments must be visible to be plotted) is false — surveying by horizon-to-horizon sighting, shadow-length, and incremental marker placement can produce long-distance straight-line alignments without any aerial view, as well-documented in Neolithic landscape archaeology.

dependencies.rationale: Builds on the triangle-alignment claim to argue for transferred extraterrestrial knowledge.

claims.text: In 1921 Alfred Watkins identified straight alignments of ancient British sites he called ley lines, which later researchers linked to Earth's magnetic field, water sources, and fault lines.

evidence.quote:
In 1921, amateur
archaeologist Alfred Watkins noticed
ancient sites formed straight alignments

claims.rationale: Watkins and his 1925 book 'The Old Straight Track' are historical fact; he did propose straight alignments. The later claimed magnetic/fault-line correlations are mostly from New Age literature, not peer-reviewed geophysics. Statistical analyses have shown that any dense set of sites produces apparent 'lines'.

claims.text: Edinburgh University scientists found stone circles often sit above underground streams, fault lines and magnetic anomalies.

evidence.quote:
Scientists at Edinburgh University found
that stone circles often sit above
underground streams and geological fault
lines

claims.rationale: The 'Dragon Project' (Paul Devereux, 1970s-80s) did measure anomalies at stone circles, but it was not an Edinburgh-University program and peer-reviewed replication is thin. The specific attribution and finding as stated is at best partially accurate.

claims.text: The megalithic yard of 0.829 m corresponds to a 413.6 Hz acoustic frequency close to the G-sharp note, suggesting the unit was harmonic in origin.

evidence.quote:
The megalithic yard is 0.829
m. A strange number unless you start
thinking in terms of harmonics

claims.rationale: The 413.6 Hz calculation uses speed-of-sound / wavelength = frequency — numerically correct if you assume the 'yard' is a wavelength, which is a huge unjustified leap. G-sharp above middle C is ~415 Hz at A440. The numerology is suggestive but circular.

claims.text: The megalithic yard of 2.72 matches Euler's number e (2.718...), the base of natural logarithms found throughout nature.

evidence.quote:
The number 2.72 or
more precisely
2.718 is the mathematical constant
euler's number.

claims.rationale: 2.72 vs 2.7182818... is a 0.06% coincidence — numerically eye-catching but almost certainly spurious given how many natural constants one could cherry-pick within 1% of any 2-significant-figure value. e's role in natural phenomena doesn't imply ancient builders discovered it.

claims.text: The megalithic yard is a remnant of knowledge inherited from a lost advanced civilization such as Atlantis.

evidence.quote:
piece of inherited knowledge
passed down from a much older, far more
advanced civilization

claims.rationale: Standard Graham Hancock / Ignatius Donnelly-style hypothesis. No archaeological, geological, or genetic evidence for a pre-Neolithic global advanced civilization exists. Mainstream archaeology has ruled this out on positive grounds of what the record does show.

claims.text: Archaeologist Clive Ruggles concluded Tom's megalithic-yard analysis suffered from selection bias — he focused on sites that fit while overlooking others.

evidence.quote:
Archaeologist Clive Rugles said Tom's
analysis suffered from selection bias.

claims.rationale: Clive Ruggles (now at Leicester) is the leading modern authority on megalithic astronomy and did publish detailed critiques of Thom's data in the 1980s-90s showing selection effects. Well-documented consensus among archaeoastronomers.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Undermines the universal-unit claim by showing Thom's selection methodology drove the apparent pattern.

claims.text: Despite decades of searching, no Neolithic measuring rod marked with a megalithic yard has ever been found, unlike Egyptian cubit rods and Mesopotamian measures.

evidence.quote:
no one has
found a Neolithic rod clearly marked
with a measurement of a megalithic yard

claims.rationale: Factually correct. Egyptian cubit rods (e.g. Turin, Louvre specimens) and Mesopotamian weights are in major museum collections; no comparable Neolithic measuring artifact exists. Absence of physical evidence is devastating to the standardized-unit hypothesis.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] No physical artifact supports the claimed precise standardized unit.

claims.text: The apparent 2.72 ft pattern is better explained as clustering around average human stride length used for rough layout measurement.

evidence.quote:
The yard might
be nothing more than the average stride
length used by builders

claims.rationale: Human stride length clusters around 2.5-3 ft depending on height and gait. Across tens of thousands of Thom measurements, natural clustering around a stride-derived unit is the parsimonious explanation without needing cosmic numerology.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] A mundane stride-clustering explanation undercuts the need for a cosmic-ruler hypothesis.

claims.text: A universal megalithic yard accurate to millimeters shared across Neolithic Europe probably did not exist — there's no physical evidence to support it.

evidence.quote:
a single universal
megalithic yard accurate to millimeters
shared across Neolithic Europe? Probably
not.

There's no physical evidence to
support it.

claims.rationale: Host's direct verdict. Aligns with mainstream archaeoastronomy consensus as articulated by Ruggles. Probability strongly against a precise universal unit given no artifact and strong selection-bias evidence.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Host concludes the universal-unit claim is probably not true.
[debunks] The lost-civilization hypothesis depends on the universal unit being real.

claims.text: Neolithic stone circles were built with intention and skill and show real astronomical alignments, confirmed by computer analyses starting in the 1960s.

evidence.quote:
the 1960s that computers confirmed
that Stonehenge and sites like it were
built with intention and great skill.

claims.rationale: Gerald Hawkins's 1963 'Stonehenge Decoded' paper and subsequent computer-assisted archaeoastronomy established the solstice and some lunar alignments beyond doubt. Neolithic builders did possess sophisticated survey skills — this portion of Thom's legacy stands.

claims.text: Julian Richards demonstrated in the 1990s that 140 people using Neolithic tools could move a 40-ton stone, showing Stonehenge-scale transport was physically possible.

evidence.quote:
archaeologist Julian Richards
demonstrated how 140 people could move a
40 ton stone using Neolithic tools.

claims.rationale: Julian Richards's 1996 BBC 'Secrets of Lost Empires' experiment is well-documented. The demonstration does not make the transport easy, but establishes it as physically achievable with Neolithic manpower and techniques — undercutting 'impossible construction' arguments.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] Demonstrated Neolithic feasibility removes the need to invoke lost-civilization technology for the construction itself.
